title: A Dose of Managed Care: Controlling Drug Spending in Medicaid
notes: Stata: reghdfe lnpriceperpresc Post_avg exp [aw=wgt], absorb(state_r yq) cluster(state_r). Post_avg=1 iff any L0-L9==1. gvar_CS = yq when L0==1 (first treatment quarter, state-level mean). LX renamed to L9 (tail bin absorbed per original Stata code). NOTE: F1==1 was previously used (one quarter before treatment) — corrected to L0==1 for correct event study alignment. CS-NT gap (-0.214 vs Stata -0.202, 5.9%) is methodological: cs_controls=[] required — xformla=~exp in att_gt causes did v2.3.0 balanced-panel code path to drop early cohorts (205-216), leaving only cohorts 220,225. Panel is balanced (29 states × 26 quarters = 754 rows); base_period='universal' is now the mandatory default (Pattern 26 revised). BJS: first_stage=~exp causes SE explosion at tau9 (SE=0.834) — bjs_controls=[] (no first_stage) gives reasonable SEs (tau9 SE~0.164) and estimates very close to Stata (tau0=-0.063 vs -0.062, tau9=-0.262 vs -0.251).

title: The Effect of Prescription Drug Monitoring Programs on Opioid Utilization in Medicare
notes: TWO SEPARATE EVENT STUDY PDFs — event_study_script.R produces: (a) event_study_com_binning.pdf (window -4 to 2, F4 bins eventhalfyear<=-4) and (b) event_study_sem_binning.pdf (window -9 to 2, separate F9-F2 dummies). CRITICAL FIX: event dummies must be 0 for never-treated states (evermustaccess==0) — otherwise never-treated states get L0=1 at halfyear=105 and dilute treatment effect by ~50%. Fix: eventhalfyear_t = ifelse(evermustaccess==1, eventhalfyear, NA). ES sample excludes NV/LA/NY/TN (es_sample_filter). Stata halfyear integers: th2012h2=105, th2012h1=104, th2011h1=102. gvar_CS: OK=102, DE/OH=104, KY/NM/WV=105, never-treated=0. MATCHES: TWFE com binning EXACT (F4=0.00067, F3=-0.00171, F2=-0.00130, L0=-0.00353); BJS EXACT (tau0=-0.00356, tau1=-0.00179, tau2=-0.00099). APPROXIMATIONS: TWFE sem L2 slightly off (R=-0.00127 vs Stata=-0.00100). SA from sunab(gvar_SA=Inf for NT). BJS via did_imputation v0.5.0 with yname/gname/tname/idname args — R package cannot estimate pre-period BJS (negative horizons silently ignored). Point estimate TWFE -0.00187 matches Stata exactly. CS-NT FIX (Round 3): Stata uses csdid2 with long2 aggregation (averages over event-time periods = dynamic). R aggte(type='simple') gave -0.00155 (14.9% gap). aggte(type='dynamic') gives -0.001888 (3.7% gap, SE=0.000859 vs Stata SE~0.00123). results.csv updated to use type='dynamic' value.

notes: Stata: reg bf_ever Post_avg $individXs $stateXs $FEs [pw=rddwt], cluster(fips). individXs=WIC_ever i.childnm i.mom_ed frstbrn i.raceeth_cat married female i.mom_agegrp. CSDID: csdid2 bf_ever i.fips $individXs [w=rddwt], agg(event) long2. TWFE FIX (Round 3 final): Two-part fix. (1) MONTH FORMULA: Stata's `gen start_month = month(start_ym)` treats %tm values as %td (days since 01jan1960, NOT months). E.g., start_ym=600 (Jan 2010 as %tm) → month(600) = 8 (Aug 1961 in %td interpretation). R corrected using `as.integer(format(as.Date('1960-01-01') + as.integer(start_ym), '%m'))`. This causes January-policy states to be correctly classified as pre-period in the year of adoption under Stata logic. (2) styr_pergt64 OMITTED: feols drops a different variable than Stata when all styr_per* are included (Stata omits styr_pergt64 due to collinearity with other proportions). Explicit removal from twfe_controls gives exact match. Combined fix: R feols = 0.03832 (exact match). CSDID-NT: 0.01652 vs Stata 0.01799 (8.2% gap). Pattern 25 applied. BJS: approximation via bjs_aggregate_to_panel. EVENT STUDY (es76_script.R): TWFE ES: EXACT MATCH with Stata (all 10 periods, gaps < 0.1%). Key: m_treat=byear-start_year (no month correction), FEs=fips+byear+childnm+mom_ed+raceeth_cat+mom_agegrp (NO agegrp — only in PE not ES), styr_pergt64 omitted. CS-NT ES: large gaps (unfixable — Stata includes i.fips as covariate causing ~50 extra state FEs, R att_gt cannot include state FEs without singularity). SA ES: 5-55% gaps, VCOV near-PSD warning (cohort identification via gvar_CS differs from Stata eventstudyinteract). BJS ES: tau2(-6.8%), tau3(+5.7%) match well; tau0/tau1 differ (aggregation vs Stata individual-level).

notes: Stata CSDID: csdid2 P_U13TDAP $individ_controls i.age i.fips [w=provwt], window(-6 5). fips = STATE dummies (51 states, NOT counties). cs_controls reduced to female+married (many controls cause singularity in att_gt). CS-NT ≈ CS-NYT in R (both ~0.095): R att_gt(panel=FALSE) distinguishes NT from NYT at ATT(g,t) cell level (62/72 cells differ), but positive and negative differences cancel in aggte(simple) aggregation — dataset-specific cancellation, not a structural bug. R NYT (~0.093) ≈ Stata NYT (0.099, ~6% diff, credible). R NT (~0.095) ≠ Stata NT (0.164) — likely due to different NT pool composition: early cohorts (MN gvar=1996, RI gvar=1999) may be in Stata's NT pool but are dropped in R as 'already treated at first period'. Stata NT has significant pre-trends (Pre_avg=-0.048, p=0.001) suggesting parallel trends failure for NT comparison; NYT is the more credible estimator (Pre_avg=-0.022, p=0.143, insignificant). See failure_patterns.md Pattern 24. BJS: Stata uses did_imputation with fe(fips year12 age); R aggregates to (fips×year12) panel via weighted.mean first — approximation (age FEs lost). title: Urban Water Disinfection and Mortality Decline in Lower-Income Countries
twfe_controls: treated_post_year; treated_year; treated; post; y; post_year
notes: DATA STRUCTURE: Each mun_reg appears TWICE per year_reg — treated2=1 (pre-reform cohort, never-treated in CS) and treated2=2 (post-reform cohort, treated in CS). (mun_reg, year_reg) is NOT unique; (mun_reg, year_reg, treated2) IS unique. 1413 unique mun_reg × 2 cohorts = 2826 composite units × 11 years. EVENT STUDY: Template's i(rel_time_binned) approach fails — when treated2 is in the FE, all event-study dummies are dropped due to collinearity (9999 bin for never-treated = treated2=1 is perfectly predicted by treated2 FE). SOLUTION: Dedicated script results/97/event_study_script.R uses explicit F_*/L_* binary dummies + fe(mun_reg + treated2 + year_reg). TWFE matches Stata exactly. CS-NT: Composite unit_cs_bjs = mun_reg*10 + treated2 + base_period='universal' — exact match for pre and post. SA = TWFE (single cohort, mathematically equivalent). GARDNER DIVERGENCE (Pattern 47): Gardner shows a constant ~0.146 level shift vs TWFE/CS-NT/SA across ALL event-study periods (pre and post). Root cause: Gardner's first stage (Gardner 2021, Section 3) estimates FEs only from untreated observations. In this dual-cohort structure, post-1991 year FEs are estimated exclusively from treated2=1 (never-treated cohort), while TWFE estimates year FEs jointly from both cohorts with treatment dummies absorbing the effect. The treated2 FE is also estimated from an asymmetric sample (all 11 years for cohort 1, only 6 pre-treatment years for cohort 2). If there is any cohort-specific time trend not captured by the additive treated2+year_reg structure (confirmed by non-zero pre-trends: +0.11 to +0.34), the first-stage counterfactual is systematically shifted. The dynamics are identical (differences between periods match perfectly), only the level differs. This is not a Gardner failure per se but a sensitivity of the two-stage approach to non-standard data structures where 'group' encompasses multiple dimensions with asymmetric untreated samples.

twfe_controls: scaprange; scappolicy; tauSCL; tauSCR; catrange; tauCATL; tauCATR; lit_urban; mean_ctrl
notes: EVENT STUDY REVIEWED. Pre-trends problematic: CS-NT/CS-NYT/SA show large negative values at h=-4,-5 (-40 to -60). Post-treatment positive but oscillating across estimators. Greenstone & Hanna (2014). Stata: xi: reg e_spm_mean scaprange scappolicy tauSCL tauSCR catrange catconvpolicy tauCATL tauCATR lit_urban mean i.city i.year [aw=pop_urban], cluster(city). Main outcome=SPM air pollution. Treatment=catalytic converter policy (staggered by city). catconvpolicy = post-adoption * within-event-window[-7,9]. Regression jointly controls for SCAP policy. Sample restricted to cities with >=3yr pre+post for BOTH policies. N=1172 (150 missing lit_urban/mean auto-dropped). Data built via build_analysis_data.R from combined.dta. tauSCR and tauCATR dropped for collinearity with city+year FEs. SE difference (R:11.93 vs Stata:12.59) due to feols singleton removal. CS-DID does NOT control for SCAP — results differ from TWFE by design. | Outcome selection: Rule (ii) largest N. Main DiD finding is energy poverty (SPM) with full county-year panel (N~45k). Mortality analysis uses a 2-step approach with only N=16 counties as units, too small for standard DiD. Audit confirmed.

title: The effects of state paid sick leave mandates on parental childcare time
twfe_controls: fam_med; pto_state; poverty; pop; female; age; age2; non_white; hisp; mar; hs; scoll; collg; grad; cohabit; hh_numkids; kids15; kids617; rural
notes: EVENT STUDY REVIEWED. Maclean & Pabilonia (2025) JHE. IMPORTANT: Paper's MAIN estimator is Gardner (did2s), NOT TWFE. TWFE is reported only in Appendix Table 10 as one of 5 alternative estimators (Gardner, BJS, Wooldridge, Stacked DiD, TWFE). Appendix Table 10 TWFE: reghdfe carehh_k pslm_state_lag2 controls [pw=wt06], a(fips time) cl(fips). RCS data (139K obs, 78K with hh_child==1). Monthly time (239 periods), 44 states, 10 treated states (6 yearly cohorts: 2014,2017,2018,2019,2020,2022). CS-DID segfaults with monthly RCS — use yearly collapsed panel for CS: CS-NT=-1.61 (SE 4.20), CS-NYT=-1.25 (SE 4.22), both insignificant. Paper uses 'carehh_k' = primary childcare minutes per average day. SIGN REVERSAL: TWFE=+4.45** vs CS-NT=-1.61 (ns). Bacon decomposition shows cohort ~2020 (ATT=+10.12, weight=34%) drives positive TWFE — confounded with COVID lockdowns (PSL lagged 2yr → 2018 adoption activates 2020). Cohorts 2017 and 2022 show negative ATTs (-3.97 and -21.49). GARDNER DIAGNOSTIC: Gardner agrees with TWFE (both positive early post-treatment, negative at late horizons). NOT sensitive to time granularity (monthly≈yearly). IS sensitive to endpoint binning: bin at h=+7 collapses h=7(+10) with h=8-9(-41) → spurious -25 drop. h≥7 is 100% Cohort 2014 (CT, N=33) — pure noise. Cohort-specific Gardner: wildly heterogeneous (2014: +16 to -57; 2017: -3 to -18; 2020: +4 to +15 with SE~10; 2022: -6 to -15). TWFE/Gardner agreement explained by shared cohort-size weighting (Cohort 2017=40%, 2020=25% dominate), while CS uses uniform group×time weighting. Conclusion: sign reversal is real — driven by COVID-confounded Cohort 2020 dominating size-weighted estimators (TWFE, Gardner) while CS distributes weight more evenly.

title: The Air Quality and Well-Being Effects of Low Emission Zones
notes: EVENT STUDY REVIEWED. Sarmiento, Wagner & Zaklan (2023) JEEM. Low emission zones in German cities reduce PM10 pollution. Staggered adoption 2008-2016. CS-DD is the paper's primary estimator (att_gt with DR, nevertreated). 588 stations, 14 years (2005-2018). Raw sample (stations inside/near LEZ). Clustering at municipality level. No covariates in CS spec (xformla=~1). Paper's TWFE uses daily data with weather controls; our TWFE is on yearly data without controls for comparability with CS-DD. EVENT STUDY (5 estimators): All converge — growing negative effect from h=0 (-0.3 to -0.9) to h=5 (-1.9 to -2.4). Pre-trends flat for TWFE/SA/Gardner; CS-NT/CS-NYT slightly noisy at far pre-periods. TWFE vs CS-NT gap (1.24 vs 1.95) is from aggte(dynamic) weighting late horizons equally. VCOV warning on SA (small cohorts 2010/2015/2016). No heterogeneity variables in data (no state/region/station_type). Script: event_study_228.R.

title: The Power of Abortion Policy: Re-examining the Effects of Young Women's Access to Reproductive Control
notes: Myers (2017) JPE. TWFE uses CONTINUOUS epillconsent18 (fraction of years 14-17 with pill consent, values 0/0.25/0.50/0.75/1.00) matching original specification. CS/SA/Gardner use DISCRETIZED binary pillconsent18 for group identification. Original multivariate spec includes 6 policy exposures + state trends — our univariate TWFE gives -0.0053 vs original -0.0084 (both insig). Pre-aggregated from 272K individual CPS obs to 49 states x 24 birth-cohorts (1935-1958) panel (weighted mean). 2 non-absorbing states (28,30) excluded. 9 treatment cohorts (1942-1956), 3 never-treated (29,31,56). Outcome: probability of first birth before age 19. EVENT STUDY REVIEWED: All 4 estimators (TWFE, CS-NT, SA, Gardner) show null results at every horizon — point estimates bounce around ±0.01, all CIs include zero. No pre-trends, no post-treatment effect. Classic null. The +350% TWFE-vs-CS ratio is meaningless (both ≈0).

title: NA

Paper uses county + state*year FEs (additional_fes). Paper restricts TWFE to rel_year in [-3,3] (line 239 of Manuscript Code.R), dropping 247 treated-county obs far from treatment. Never-treated have rel_year=0 (always in window). Without filter: TWFE=-33.65 (6.76% gap). With filter: TWFE=-31.52 (exact match). CS segfaults on filtered sample (unbalanced panel: treated 4-7yrs, never-treated 9yrs triggers did pkg C code crash). STANDALONE SCRIPT required: investigate_241.R runs TWFE on filtered data, CS/SA/BJS on full data. Template cannot handle separate TWFE/CS samples. No controls in main spec. Outcome = MME per capita * 10. Table 1 Col 1. Replication code is R (not Stata). Original code: data/241/Files for Replication/Programs/AEJPol-2023-0640 Manuscript Code.R.

title: The Unintended Consequences of Infrastructure Development
notes: EVENT STUDY REVIEWED. Gardner added — sits between SA (+1.36) and TWFE (+2.21) as expected. All 5 estimators show growing positive effect. Bancalari (2024). REStat. Table 2 Panel A Col 1: reghdfe vs_imr1y D, a(i t) cluster(i). TWFE coef = +0.74 (SE=0.42, p=0.08). 1,467 districts, 2005-2015. Staggered: sewerage project initiation across 11 cohorts (2005-2015). 22% never-treated (326 districts). CS uses notyet control group in paper (csdid ... notyet agg(event) long2). No time-varying controls in main spec. Event study window -6 to +7 (trimmed from -10/+10). Use analysis_district_clean_all.dta (NOT analysis_district_clean.dta). gvar_CS = Ei for treated (0 for never-treated). Paper finds CS ATT = +1.79 vs TWFE +0.74 (TWFE attenuated). TWFE exact match (0.737 vs 0.74). CS-NYT gap (R=2.77 dynamic, paper=1.79): ATT(g,t) individuais divergem radicalmente entre R native e pair-balanced — coortes tardias (2014-2015) têm valores extremos no R native (-8.6, -6.4) que puxam a agregação. R allow_unbalanced_panel=TRUE é a fonte principal — com 34% de dados faltantes (355/1467 unidades com painel completo), o pacote faz ajustes internos que divergem do Stata. Base period importa para o pré: varying (default do Stata) dá padrão de sinais diferente de universal (nosso default). Overall ATT pós-tratamento: pair-balanced NYT = 1.78 ≈ paper 1.79 (match perfeito). A divergência é exclusiva do pré-tratamento. Cohort 2005 (78 units) dropped by R att_gt (treated in first period, no pre-treatment base with universal). BJS fails (subscript out of bounds).

title: The Effect of Medicaid Reimbursement Rates on Nursing Home Staffing

We create binary treatment: high-Medicaid IL facilities (342 CCNs, above-median Medicaid share) = treated, low-Medicaid IL facilities (308 CCNs) = control. title: Informed Enforcement: Lessons from Pollution Monitoring in China
notes: EVENT STUDY REVIEWED. CS-NT CIs 7x wider than TWFE — structural: can't absorb industry×time + province×time FEs (Pattern 50). Tested: ind dummies (531) in xformla → overfitting (ATT=0); prov dummies (21) → SE worse. Point estimates agree (TWFE=0.0033, CS=0.0026). CS-NYT not applicable (single timing). Axbard & Deng (2023). Table 1a Col 1. Stata: reghdfe any_air c.min_dist_10#c.post1 if min_dist<50 & starty<=2010, absorb(time id industry#time prov_id#time) cluster(city_id). Treatment = firm within 10km of air monitor (min_dist_10) x post-2015 (post1). Single treatment date: Q1 2015 (time=21). 36103 firms (after filter), 32 quarters (2010-2017). Outcome: any air enforcement action (0/1). FE: firm + time + industry*time + province*time. Cluster: city_id (171 cities). SA disabled (single treatment). CS-NYT disabled (single treatment). title: Maternal Mortality and Women's Political Power
notes: EVENT STUDY REVIEWED. Bhalotra, Clarke, Gomes, Venkataramani (2022) JEEA. Table from quotaDifDif.tex, Col 4 (lnMMRt1, no controls). Stata: xtreg lnMMRt1 i.year quotaRes, fe cluster(ccode). 178 countries, 26 years (1990-2015). 22 reserved-seat quota countries with staggered adoption 1989-2013. Outcome: ln(Maternal Mortality Ratio). Treatment: quotaRes (binary reserved seats indicator). No controls in baseline. Paper also uses did_multiplegt_dc and HonestDiD. Uganda adopted 1989 (pre-sample start). Panel unbalanced: 119/178 have all 26 years. EVENT STUDY (5 estimators): All estimators agree on gradual negative effect growing from h=0 (-0.012) to h=5 (-0.09 to -0.15). Pre-trends flat across all. CS-NT/CS-NYT run on balanced panel (119 countries, att_gt segfaults on unbalanced with 13 cohorts). CS-NT overall=-0.169, CS-NYT=-0.170. SA most conservative at late horizons (-0.089 at h=5); Gardner largest (-0.147 at h=5). TWFE attenuation confirmed by Bacon: early adopters negative, late adopters positive. VCOV warning on SA (few treated clusters). Controls (lnGDP, democracy) tested — pre-trends already flat in baseline, no improvement needed. Scripts: event_study_267.R (TWFE+SA+Gardner), cs_only_267.R (CS-NT+CS-NYT on balanced panel), plot_final_267.R (combined 5-estimator plot).

title: The Curse of Plenty: The Green Revolution and the Rise in Chronic Disease

Paper's main result is health (IHDS data not provided), this is the first-stage agricultural effect. | Outcome selection: Rule (ii) largest N. Audit confirmed. | HonestDiD CS-NT infeasible: single treatment cohort (1966) with base_period='universal' (mandatory) causes all pre-treatment CS-DID ATTs to have SE=NA, making HonestDiD impossible. TWFE-only HonestDiD available.

notes: Mass shootings and local economic outcomes. 173 treated counties (2000-2013), ~2938 never-treated. Treatment = 'successful' (=1 post-shooting). This is Col 3 spec (beta=-1.348, SE=0.523), NOT Col 1 (beta=-2.658). CS-DID SEGFAULT FIX: att_gt segfaults on the aroundms-filtered sample (both panel=TRUE unbalanced and panel=FALSE RCS) due to 3111 fips with extreme imbalance. Fix: use balanced panel (2613 fips with all 24 years, of which 127 treated + 2486 never-treated) WITHOUT aroundms filter. TWFE/SA/Gardner still use aroundms filter (as paper specifies). D_ = baseline×trend (Pattern 37), R_/H_ = time-varying. 10 cohorts (1965-1974). SA applicable. Drops NYC/LA/Chicago. CS-NT DIAGNOSTICS (Pattern 51): Baseline CS-NT (xformla=~1) showed +40-50 pre-trend bias vs TWFE≈0. Root cause: identification relies on Durb×year + state×year FEs that CS-NT can't absorb. Tested: (1) xformla=~Durb_f (5 urban categories) → pre-trends reduced ~80% to +10-15, ATT=-59 (SE=20); (2) + state dummies → overshoot to negative pre-trends (Pattern 42 overfitting). Fix: cs_controls=[Durb_f]. 5 urban dummies capture most of the confounding without propensity overfitting. Remaining gap vs TWFE due to state×year + baseline×trend controls that only regression-based estimators absorb.

title: Learning about an Infrequent Event: Evidence from Flood Insurance Take-Up in the United States
notes: Gallagher (2014), AEJ: Applied Economics. Outcome selection: Rule (i) declared preference — equation (1) is described as 'the main estimating equation' (p.214). Outcome: ln_takeup_proxy1 = log(flood insurance policies per capita + 1). Figure 2 event study on 1990-2007 panel. Collapse = 1: paper reports ONLY event study coefficients (no static ATT), so we construct post_hit = 1{year2 >= hit1year} for static TWFE. Unit = NFIP community (id_num, N=10841). Treatment = first 'hit' by a PDD flood. hit1year = calendar year of first hit (NA for 3927 never-hit communities). 18 staggered cohorts (1990-2007), 6914 treated communities, 3927 never-treated. FEs: community + state*year (st_fips^year2, 864 categories). No time-varying controls. Cluster: state (st_fips, 48 states). IDIOSYNCRASY — REPEATED EVENTS: The paper's Figure 2 uses hityear_* variables that encode event-time dummies relative to ALL PDD floods, not just the first. 55% of treated communities (3822/6914) are hit >1 time (distribution: 1 hit=3092, 2=2278, 3=992, 4=385, 5+=163). This means hityear_p5 = 1 whenever a community is 5 years after ANY flood, not just the first. Standard staggered DiD (first-hit i(rel_time_binned)) gives systematically HIGHER post-treatment coefficients because all subsequent flood effects are absorbed into the first-hit timing. TWFE ES coefficients: first-hit t=0: 0.090, repeated-event t=0: 0.078 (Fig 2 match). At late horizons the gap grows: first-hit t=10: 0.094, repeated-event t=10: 0.024 (4x ratio). ES SCRIPT: es432_script.R uses raw hityear_* variables for TWFE ES (exact Figure 2 match) and first-hit for CS/SA/Gardner (standard staggered DiD). This mismatch is documented: the TWFE reflects the paper's specification while CS/SA/Gardner apply standard modern DiD methodology. The do-file uses pre-demeaned variables (_dmn suffix) because Stata areg absorbs only one FE set. In fixest, raw hityear_* + | id_num + st_fips^year2 gives EXACT match (all 22 coefficients identical to 5 decimal places). Panel: 195138 rows, balanced (10841 x 18 years). gvar_CS uses as.numeric() per Pattern 43; never-treated = 0. base_period = 'universal' per Pattern 26.

title: Life and Death in the Fast Lane: Police Enforcement and Traffic Fatalities
notes: DeAngelo & Hansen (2014), AEJ: Economic Policy. Oregon State Police mass layoff Feb 1, 2003 (single-timing DiD). State-level monthly panel, 47 states (excl AK, HI, VT), 2000-2005. Treatment: Oregon (fips=41) from Feb 2003 (mn_yr=38); Jan 2003 coded as pre-treatment since layoff was Feb 1. Outcome: fat_vmt = traffic fatalities per VMT (all highways, all weather conditions). OUTCOME SELECTION: Protocol rule (ii) largest N selects full US sample (N=3384, Table 4 col 1) over geographic control WA+ID (N=216, col 2). Rule (iii.a) selects fat_vmt (aggregate) over fat_good_vmt (outside city/dry weather subcategory). Original beta_twfe=0.7103 is the raw coefficient; paper reports semi-elasticity=0.05 (=0.7103/13.00 mean). DATA FILE: original fatal_analysis_file_2014.dta is MISSING from replication package. Using synth_file_2014.dta which contains identical variables for all 47 states; treatment variable is already coded in the data. This file has 47 states (missing VT fips=50) vs paper's 48 (N=3456 vs 3384, difference of 1 state). Stata: areg fat_vmt treatment mon_* y_* precip temp un_rate max_speed, a(fips) robust cluster(state). FE structure: fips absorbed by areg, year+month dummies as covariates = equivalent to feols(... | fips + year + month). Clustering: state-level for full US sample (fips is 1:1 with state). Paper also reports WA+ID geographic control (robust SE, no clustering) and synthetic control (5 states with weights). CS-DID note: only 1 treated unit (Oregon) with 46 never-treated states; att_gt should work but variance estimation may be imprecise with single treated unit. SA trivial for single timing but included for consistency. Event study: monthly data with 37 pre-periods and 35 post-periods relative to Feb 2003; event_pre/post set to 6 for readable plot (6 months each side). No run_csdid_nyt because there are never-treated states available (run_csdid_nt instead). No Bacon decomposition because single timing (no heterogeneous treatment timing).

title: Corporate Incentives and Nuclear Safety
notes: SIGN REVERSAL DIAGNOSED (Pattern 46): CS-NT=+40.4 and CS-NYT=+38.2 vs TWFE=-42.2. Root cause: (a) massive secular decline in personrem (1200→135 over 1974-2008), (b) very long pre-treatment (25yr before earliest cohort 1999), (c) unbalanced panel (14/63 facilities have full span), (d) 3 singleton cohorts with extreme leverage. CS with universal base period mixes high-value 1970s-80s with low-value 1990s, creating composition effects. When restricting to 1990-2008, all estimators converge to near-zero. ALL estimates are statistically insignificant (SEs~60-70). TWFE=-42.245 matches Stata exactly. CS estimates are unreliable — do not include in aggregate analysis. | Hausman (2014), AEJ-EP. Nuclear plant divestiture and safety outcomes. STAGGERED DiD: 30 facilities divested 1999-2007, 33 never-divested facilities as controls. 63 facilities total, annual panel 1974-2008. | OUTCOME SELECTION: Protocol rule (i) declared preference for OLS-compatible outcome. Paper's main results (Tables 3-5) use Poisson/NB for count outcomes (initiating events, fires, escalated enforcement) -- NOT OLS-compatible for our feols template. Table 6 reports OLS regressions for radiation exposure: personrem (collective dose, person-rem), rem (average dose), and capacity-factor-normalized variants (n_personrem, n_rem). personrem chosen per rule (iii.a): aggregate measure (collective plant dose) over per-worker average (rem). personrem has N=1749 facility-year obs, same as rem. | SPECIFICATION: Stata do-file line 971: reg personrem divested R* Y*, cluster(plantid). R* = facility FEs (tab facilityname, gen(R)), Y* = year FEs (Y1-Y40 from prior tab). No controls. Cluster at facility level (plantid = facilityname encoded as integer). | DATA CONSTRUCTION: Raw data is reactor x month panel (safetypanel.dta, 36667 obs). Radiation regression collapses to facility x year: drop if rem==., then collapse (mean) personrem rem divested ... by(facilityname year ...). personrem is annual plant-level data (same across months and reactors within facility-year). Divested is binary at monthly level but becomes fractional in divestiture year after collapsing (18 obs with 0 < divested < 1). Template uses the fractional divested as treatment_twfe (matching Stata exactly). gvar_CS uses year_divest (year of divestiture) for CS-DID. | NOTE: TWFE coefficient -42.245 (SE 66.098) is not statistically significant. The paper's main finding is from Poisson regressions on count outcomes (positive effect on unsafe events). The OLS radiation result is a secondary finding. | 9 treatment cohorts (1999: 2, 2000: 7, 2001: 9, 2002: 3, 2003: 1, 2004: 1, 2005: 4, 2006: 1, 2007: 2) + 33 never-treated. Enough cohorts for SA. Event study: pre=5, post=5 matches do-file event dummies (pre_5_plus through post_5_plus, lines 1471-1514). | POTENTIAL ISSUE: The fractional divested (treatment_twfe) may cause slight differences vs binary CS-DID treatment (gvar_CS defines treatment as year >= year_divest). This is inherent to the Stata specification which collapses monthly binary to annual fractional.

title: Pensions and Fertility: Microeconomic Evidence
notes: Brazil pension reform July 1991. First post-reform PNAD is 1992. | RCS design - PNAD cross-sections. No individual panel ID. | TREAT = rural worker (rw_p5==1), control = urban worker (uwb2==1). | DID = TREAT * AFTER (pre-constructed). AFTER = syear >= 1992. | No PNAD in 1991 or 1994 - gaps in time series. | No weights used in original regressions. | Single treatment date - all treated units get treatment in 1992. | Table 3 Col 2: DID = -0.008 (SE 0.003), N = 1,442,376. | 26 state clusters (clustervar1).

title: Modern Medicine and the 20th Century Decline in Mortality
notes: Jayachandran, Lleras-Muney & Smith (2010) AEJ:Applied. Disease-time DiD: treated diseases (MMR) vs control (TB) before/after sulfa drugs 1937. State-level panel 1925-1943. Original Stata: areg lnm_rate treatedXpost37 treatedXyear_c treated year_c, absorb(statepost) cluster(diseaseyear). Our TWFE uses unit(state_disease)+year FEs instead of statepost FEs. treatedXyear_c included as control (differential trend for treated diseases). Primary result: Table 4 Panel B Column 1 (MMR vs TB). Single treatment time (1937) → no SA. CS-DID with gvar=1937 for MMR states, 0 for TB. Cluster: disease×year (as in original). Disease 1=MMR (treated), 4=TB (control).

title: The Distortionary Effects of Incentives in Government: Evidence from China's Death Ceiling Program
notes: Data prepared by results/1094/prep_data_1094.R from wide-format data_aej.dta. | Outcome: I(Deaths/Ceiling > 1) — binary LPM. | Treatment: NSNP law adoption, staggered across 19 provinces (2005-2012). | post = I(year > effective_year) — strict inequality, so gvar_CS = effective_year + 1. | 12 truly never-treated + 4 with effective_year=2012 (treated after sample) = 16 control provinces. | Unit for panel: provinceind = province x industry (245 units). FEs: provinceind + year + industry. | Paper Table 2 Col 1: -0.051 (0.040), N=1755. R TWFE: -0.047 (0.041), N=1756. | Small N gap: Stata drops 1 obs during merge that R doesn't. 8% coefficient gap from merge/processing diffs. | Clustering at province level (31 clusters).

title: Mitigating agricultural fires with carrot or stick? Evidence from China
twfe_controls: temperature; precipitation; windspeed; humidity; winddirection1; winddirection2; winddirection3
notes: Data prepared by results/2303/prep_data_2303.R from biomass_plant_year_month_MCD12Q1.dta + plant_weather.dta. | Outcome: ft90 — fire frequency count within 0-90 km radius of BPP (main Table 2 outcome). | Treatment: tbp = 1 if biomass power plant is operational in year-month. Binary, absorbing, staggered. | Time variable: ym = (year-2001)*12 + month. Monthly data 2001m1 to 2019m12 (228 periods). | gvar_CS: entry month of BPP operation (125 unique cohorts). Never-treated = 0 (487 plants). | 954 total plants: 467 treated (operational by 2019), 487 never-treated. | TWFE FE override: id^month (plant×calendar month) + prov^year^month (province×year×month). | Paper uses Conley spatial SE (600km bandwidth). Template uses cluster by plant id. | Table 2 Col 3: paper reports -4.863 (1.780 Conley). R TWFE: -4.836 (1.495 cluster). Coefficient ~0.6% gap. | CS-DID uses simple unit+time FEs (no interaction FEs). Weather controls NOT in cs_controls (Pattern 42). | SA uses sunab(gvar_CS, ym). Gardner uses did2s with same FE structure. | Event study: es_2303.R replicates Fig. 4 (k=10) with TWFE, CS-NT, SA, Gardner. | Event study uses YEARLY event time: year_month = first treated year per (id, month) pair. | Paper uses did_imputation (Borusyak 2022). Gardner replicates this; pre-trends flat (~0). | CS-NT diverges at late horizons (Pattern 38: shrinking not-yet-treated control). | TWFE and SA converge well; all estimators confirm significant negative post-treatment effect.
